items.correct: The value of the next-best alternative foregone when a choice is made — the real cost of a decision in terms of what you give up.
items.misconception: Opportunity cost is the total of everything you give up when making a choice.
items.random_wrong: Opportunity cost is the money price of something.
items.why: Opportunity cost is the NEXT BEST alternative, not the sum of all alternatives. If you can do A, B or C and choose A, the opportunity cost is whichever of B or C was your second choice — not both.

items.q: Why do economists generally argue against protectionism?
items.correct: Tariffs and quotas raise prices for consumers, protect inefficient industries, invite retaliation, and reduce overall welfare — even when they save specific jobs, the cost per job saved typically exceeds the wage.
items.misconception: Protectionism always makes a country richer by keeping money at home.
items.random_wrong: Protectionism means protecting national parks from development.
items.why: Trade is positive-sum in standard theory — countries gain from specialisation. Students assume tariffs trap wealth domestically, missing that consumers pay higher prices and exporters lose from retaliation.

items.correct: A sustained rise in the general price level over time — reducing the purchasing power of money. Measured by the percentage change in a price index.
items.misconception: Inflation is when prices are high.
items.random_wrong: Inflation is when the economy grows too fast.
items.why: Inflation is about RISING prices, not high prices. A country can have stable high prices (no inflation) or rising low prices (high inflation). Students confuse level with rate of change.

items.q: Alfred Marshall published 'Principles of Economics' establishing supply-demand analysis in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1890. “1776” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A fall in the price of a substitute good causes demand to:
items.why: Correct answer: Decrease (curve shifts left). “Increase (shifts right)” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 1890 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
